metadata.description: Held-out negotiation value_creation pairs for probing only. NOT used in vector extraction. Same domain (negotiation) as extraction pairs but entirely new scenarios. Tests whether the value_creation vector generalises within negotiation contexts.

dimensions.name: Value Creation
dimensions.description: Expanding the set of possible agreements by introducing new variables and trading across multiple issues; moving beyond zero-sum thinking

pairs.context: A client says they cannot afford the full project fee but won't accept a reduced scope.
pairs.positive: What if we structured this as two phases? Phase one now, at a price that works for your current budget, with phase two scheduled in six months when your budget resets. You get the full outcome — just over a longer timeline.
pairs.negative: If the fee doesn't work and scope can't be reduced, I'm not sure how we get to a deal. Let's see if there's anything you can cut.

pairs.context: A supplier wants a higher unit price; you need a lower one.
pairs.positive: What if we introduce volume commitment into this? If I commit to a minimum annual order, does that change what's possible on unit price? We could be creating a deal that's better for both of us than the current spot-price conversation.
pairs.negative: I need the lower unit price and you need a higher one. That's a direct conflict. Where's your floor?

pairs.context: Two parties are stuck on the split of a joint venture profit, each wanting more.
pairs.positive: Rather than arguing over a fixed split of the current pie, what if we tied the split to milestones that grow the pie? If the venture hits targets, you get a higher percentage — which means the dollar amounts still work for both of us even if the percentages look different.
pairs.negative: We both want a bigger share and there's only 100% to divide. One of us is going to have to move.

pairs.context: A buyer can't meet the asking price for a property but the seller needs to close quickly.
pairs.positive: What if the seller's timeline is actually something we can work with? If they need a fast close, I can offer speed and certainty in exchange for a lower price. We're not just negotiating a number — we're trading certainty for price, and both sides might prefer that.
pairs.negative: If the buyer can't meet the price, the seller should either wait for another buyer or lower the price.

pairs.context: An employee wants a pay rise but the company has a pay freeze on salary.
pairs.positive: Salary is frozen but that's not the only form of compensation. What about an enhanced annual leave allowance, a guaranteed bonus tied to targets, or a commitment to a title change and market-rate salary review when the freeze lifts? Let's build a package that works even within the constraint.
pairs.negative: If salary is frozen, there's not much that can be done right now. Let's revisit when the freeze is lifted.

pairs.context: A distributor wants exclusivity; you need guaranteed sales volumes.
pairs.positive: What if exclusivity is the answer to both our problems? You get exclusivity in exchange for committing to a minimum annual volume that makes giving it up worthwhile for me. Exclusivity against performance — that aligns our incentives completely.
pairs.negative: Exclusivity is expensive for us. If you want it, you'll need to pay a premium.

pairs.context: A candidate wants a flexible work arrangement; the role traditionally requires full-time presence.
pairs.positive: What if we designed the role around outputs rather than presence? You commit to specific deliverables and availability windows, and in return get the flexibility you need. We might actually get more from this arrangement than the traditional model.
pairs.negative: The role is full-time on-site by design. If that's a dealbreaker, this may not be the right fit.

pairs.context: A landlord and tenant are stuck on rent — too high for the tenant, too low for the landlord at current terms.
pairs.positive: Let's bring more variables into this. What if a longer lease term, say five years, justifies a lower monthly rate? You get security and lower costs; I get certainty and no re-letting risk. Neither of us gets exactly what we named, but both of us might end up better off.
pairs.negative: One of you is going to need to move on the rent. The gap needs to close somehow.

pairs.context: A client wants a longer project timeline; you need the revenue sooner.
pairs.positive: What if we structured billing at the start of each phase rather than at completion? You get the longer timeline you need to manage delivery; I get the cash flow timing that works for my business. We're solving two different problems with one structure change.
pairs.negative: If the timeline extends, the cash flow impact on my side is a real problem. I'd need more upfront to offset that.

pairs.context: Two firms are splitting the work on a contract and both want the more profitable components.
pairs.positive: Let's map out everything in this contract — revenue, cost, and the non-financial value like client relationship and IP. If we're honest about what each component is worth to each firm, we might find a split that looks unequal on paper but is actually better for both sides.
pairs.negative: Both firms want the high-margin work. We'll need to split those components 50-50 and each take some of the lower-margin work to make it fair.

pairs.context: A job candidate wants equity; the startup can't give up more at this stage.
pairs.positive: What if we converted some of the salary ask into a different kind of stake — maybe an option pool tied to a future round, rather than current equity? You get meaningful upside without the current dilution being the mechanism.
pairs.negative: We can't dilute further right now. The salary is the salary and the equity is what it is.

pairs.context: A buyer wants better product quality but won't pay more.
pairs.positive: What if better quality is actually achievable without a higher unit price if the order size changes? Larger runs reduce our variable cost and let us invest more in materials per unit. Volume and quality might move in the same direction here.
pairs.negative: Better quality costs more to produce. If you want it, the price has to reflect that.

pairs.context: A supplier relationship is under strain because of late payments on one side and unreliable delivery on the other.
pairs.positive: What if we tied the two problems together? You commit to consistent delivery timelines and we commit to faster payment. Instead of each complaining about the other's failure, we create a structure that rewards both sides for performing.
pairs.negative: Your delivery issues and our payment issues are separate problems. Let's deal with them one at a time.

pairs.context: A co-founder wants to work fewer hours; the other needs full commitment.
pairs.positive: What if reduced hours came with a specific role — not fewer responsibilities spread across everything, but a defined domain where that person retains full accountability? The company gets clarity; they get boundaries. It might work better than what we have now.
pairs.negative: Fewer hours means less contribution. If the equity split doesn't change, that's not a deal I can accept.

pairs.context: A buyer wants faster delivery; a seller needs time to produce at the agreed quality.
pairs.positive: What if some of the lead time is actually in shipping and handling rather than production? If the buyer can pre-approve the design stage before production finishes, we save the back-and-forth time at the end. We might be able to get closer to your timeline without compromising quality.
pairs.negative: Faster delivery means either lower quality or higher cost. You'll need to accept one of those tradeoffs.
